summary.executiveSummary: The escrow contract implements a simple two-party payment flow for audit jobs with correct object-consumption semantics (no double-spend possible). However, the payer retains unilateral refund rights at all times with no time-lock or completion proof, making it impossible for the authority to guarantee payment after delivering work. Additionally, the payer freely sets the authority address at lock time with no on-chain verification, allowing self-dealing. The status field is dead code, and zero-value escrows are unchecked.

findings.title: Payer retains unconditional refund rights — authority cannot guarantee payment
findings.description: Both payer and authority may call refund() at any time. There is no state transition that locks out the payer once audit work has begun or completed. After delivering audit results, the authority calls capture(), but the payer can race with — or preemptively submit — a refund() transaction. Because AuditEscrow is a shared object, both transactions are sequenced by validators; whichever executes first wins. The payer has a strong financial incentive to front-run capture after receiving results, giving them audited output for free.
findings.impact: Payer can reclaim funds after audit delivery by racing refund() against capture(). Authority bears full counterparty risk with no on-chain remedy.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a two-phase or time-locked commit: (1) add a STATUS_CAPTURED or STATUS_RELEASED state; (2) add an authority-only mark_complete() that flips status and starts a dispute window; (3) only allow payer refund before mark_complete() or after a timeout. Alternatively require the authority to sign capture and add a deadline after which payer may reclaim, creating a fair race window only during the dispute period.
findings.codeSnippet:
assert!(sender == escrow.payer || sender == escrow.authority, ENotAuthorized);
// payer and authority have identical refund rights — no completion gate

findings.title: Payer controls authority address — self-dealing bypasses escrow intent
findings.description: The authority address is supplied by the payer at lock() time with no on-chain validation. A payer can pass their own address (or any address they control) as authority. In that case, capture() transfers funds back to the payer, making the escrow a no-op. There is also no check that authority != payer.
findings.impact: Payer can create a fake escrow that never actually exposes funds to a real auditor, potentially for UI spoofing, off-chain fraud, or fee gaming.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) restrict lock() to accept only a whitelisted authority address stored in a shared registry/admin cap, or (b) assert authority != payer inside lock(). If the protocol requires open authority selection, document the off-chain verification path explicitly.

findings.title: status field is dead code — false impression of state-machine enforcement
findings.description: STATUS_LOCKED is assigned on object creation and is never read or asserted in capture() or refund(). Both functions destructure the object via pattern matching (status: _), ignoring the field entirely. The constant STATUS_LOCKED = 0 and the status: u8 field exist but provide zero enforcement. Developers reading the code may believe state transitions are enforced when they are not.
findings.impact: No direct exploit, but the misleading structure can cause future developers to omit status checks when adding new states, or assume guards exist where they do not.
findings.recommendation: Either remove the status field and STATUS_LOCKED constant entirely (simplifying the struct), or implement actual state assertions in capture/refund that check and transition the status field.

findings.title: Zero-amount escrow not prevented
findings.description: lock() does not assert amount > 0. A zero-value Coin<T> is valid in Move. This allows creation of AuditEscrow objects with no funds, polluting shared-object state, emitting misleading EscrowLocked events with amount=0, and potentially tricking off-chain indexers or UIs into displaying active escrows that hold nothing.
findings.impact: Spam/dust escrow objects; off-chain accounting confusion; wasted storage deposits.
findings.recommendation: Add assert!(amount > 0, EZeroAmount) immediately after computing amount in lock().
findings.codeSnippet:
let amount = coin::value(&payment);
// missing: assert!(amount > 0, EZeroAmount);

findings.title: Redundant amount field diverges from funds balance if contract is extended
findings.description: The struct stores both funds: Balance<T> and amount: u64. The amount field is set once at lock time and only appears in events. The public amount() accessor correctly reads balance::value(&escrow.funds) rather than escrow.amount. If future functions add or remove from funds (partial capture, fee splits), the amount field would silently diverge from the real balance, causing incorrect event data.
findings.impact: No current exploit. Risk surface grows if the contract is extended with partial-capture or fee logic.
findings.recommendation: Remove the amount: u64 field and compute it on-the-fly from balance::value(&escrow.funds) at event emission. This eliminates the invariant maintenance burden.
findings.codeSnippet:
funds: Balance<T>,
amount: u64,  // redundant — derivable from balance::value(&funds)

findings.title: Shared object creation imposes sequencing overhead for every escrow
findings.description: transfer::share_object() makes AuditEscrow a consensus-sequenced shared object. Every subsequent transaction (capture, refund) must go through Sui's consensus layer rather than the fast-path single-owner route. For a bilateral two-party flow where only payer and authority interact, an owned-object model (transfer to authority) or wrapping pattern would be cheaper and faster.
findings.impact: Higher latency and gas cost per escrow lifecycle. At scale this compounds across many concurrent audits.
findings.recommendation: Consider transferring the escrow object to the authority as an owned object. The authority can then execute capture or co-sign refund. If the dispute/refund-by-payer flow is essential, evaluate a programmable transaction block pattern or a two-owner witness instead of shared-object consensus.
findings.codeSnippet: transfer::share_object(escrow);  // forces consensus sequencing on all future ops

expensivePatterns: transfer::share_object() on every lock() — all downstream capture/refund calls require consensus sequencing instead of fast-path execution
Storing redundant amount: u64 alongside Balance<T> wastes 8 bytes of on-chain storage per escrow object
optimizationSuggestions: Use owned-object transfer to authority instead of share_object to enable fast-path execution for capture/refund
Remove amount: u64 field; compute from balance::value at event emission time
Batch EscrowLocked event data — escrow_id is re-derived from &escrow after creation; derive it once and reuse

overallRecommendations: CRITICAL PATH: Add a completion gate — authority-only mark_complete() with a payer-dispute window — before any mainnet deployment. Without it the escrow does not guarantee payment.
Validate that authority != payer and optionally restrict authority to a whitelisted registry to prevent self-dealing.
Remove the dead status field and STATUS_LOCKED constant, or implement real state-machine transitions with assertions.
Assert amount > 0 in lock() to prevent zero-value spam escrows.
Consider migrating to an owned-object model (escrow held by authority) to eliminate consensus overhead on every capture/refund call.
